questions.question: What happens if you call two terminal operations on the same stream?
options: The second one returns an empty result
IllegalStateException — stream has already been operated upon
The stream resets and runs the pipeline again
questions.explanation: Streams are single-use. After a terminal operation consumes the stream, any subsequent operation throws IllegalStateException. Create a new stream from the source if needed.

questions.question: What does `Stream.of(1,2,3).map(n -> n * 2).peek(System.out::println)` print?
options: Nothing — there is no terminal operation
questions.explanation: Streams are lazy. Without a terminal operation (like collect, forEach, count), intermediate operations (map, peek) are never executed.

questions.question: Which collector groups elements by a classifier and counts each group?
questions.explanation: groupingBy with counting() as a downstream collector produces a Map<K, Long> with the count for each group key.

questions.question: What is the difference between `stream.toList()` and `stream.collect(Collectors.toList())`?
options: toList() returns an unmodifiable list; Collectors.toList() returns a mutable ArrayList
toList() is faster but less type-safe
Collectors.toList() returns an unmodifiable list; toList() returns mutable
questions.explanation: Stream.toList() (Java 16+) returns an unmodifiable list that does not allow null. Collectors.toList() returns a mutable ArrayList. Choose based on whether you need mutability.

questions.question: When should you use `flatMap` instead of `map`?
options: When you want to filter elements
When each element maps to multiple elements (the mapping function returns a Stream)
When you need to sort the results
When the source is a parallel stream
questions.explanation: flatMap flattens nested structures. If map would produce Stream<Stream<T>> or Stream<List<T>>, use flatMap to flatten into Stream<T>. Example: flatMap(list -> list.stream()).
